recent_lessons: Johnson's PTS miss (actual 14 vs T15) in the same blowout context is borderline variance/model_gap_rule: a 9/10 player at T15 missing by 1 point in a 22-point blowout loss is not a pure selection error, but the blowout-loss context (ATL trailing by large margins in the second half) structurally reduces the primary forward's scoring volume — the model's blowout penalty for PTS should account for losing-side players, not just winning-side star-rest scenarios.

AST props at T6 and above carry meaningful single-game variance (Johnson's sigma=0.366 was flagged in trim_reasons) — the 74% confidence on an 8/10 AST T6 was reasonable in isolation, but the combination of volatile AST, road underdog status, and blowout risk created a correlated downside that the individual pick confidence did not fully price. When two or more of these factors co-occur on an AST prop at T6+, apply an additional 5pp discount or require 9+/10 hit rate to proceed.

LeBron PTS missed at 12 vs. a T15 floor with a 9/10 hit rate (95%) — the root cause is the Without-Luka baseline (6-game sample, n=6) being used when Luka was QUES not confirmed OUT. Post-game news confirms Luka was OUT (confirmed absence listed in YESTERDAY'S NOTABLE ABSENCES), meaning the baseline was structurally valid, but the actual output of 12 PTS (34 min) still missed a T15 floor in a 1-point game (LAL 105, ORL 104). In an extremely close game that came down to the final possession, LeBron's scoring volume was likely managed and minutes-efficient rather than volume-heavy — the CLOSE game script (margin=1) did not suppress minutes but may have compressed possessions in late-game defense-first scenarios. The 9/10 historical hit rate is sound, but the combination of (a) a single-digit-possession game script, (b) a 6-game Without-Luka baseline sample (small), and (c) a tough ORL defense rating on PTS (opp=mid, not soft) represents a convergence of marginal risks the model treated independently. A tighter rule requiring confirmed OUT (not just QUES) before applying the Without-Star PTS baseline would have either voided this pick or reduced confidence below the 80% threshold.

Kawhi Leonard REB missed at 3 vs. a pick of 4 with a 9/10 hit rate and iron-floor=true at T4 — this is a variance miss given the tier and hit rate, but the trend=down signal on REB was present and the analyst applied only a −5% confidence penalty rather than considering whether a rebounding pick in a competitive-to-blowout game (LAC won by 7, a CLOSE game) warranted greater scrutiny. The tier_walk notes a T4 exact-match exemption but the Without-Garland baseline at T4=55% (below threshold) was flagged and the standard T4 was used instead — this is the correct path per rules, but it means the qualifying tier was the standard baseline, not a verified Without-Garland line. REB at the minimum valid tier (T4) with trend=down and a QUES designation is a structurally thin pick even with iron-floor status.

Julius Randle PTS MISS (9 vs threshold 10): The Without-Edwards 10/10 hit rate at T15 (corrected to T10) was genuinely elite historical evidence, but a 10.5-point road underdog situation at BOS creates losing-side game-script suppression that compressed Randle's scoring volume below even the T10 floor. MIN lost by 10 (COMPETITIVE borderline), and with 29 minutes played, Randle's scoring was simply squeezed — he finished at 9, one unit below threshold. This is a model_gap_rule miss: the losing-side blowout PTS penalty exists in the rules but was not applied aggressively enough given a spread_abs of 10.5. The pick's tier_walk noted the Without-Edwards elevation but did not apply a full -10% losing-side PTS penalty, which would have pushed confidence below the selection threshold or triggered a tier step-down. Lesson: when spread_abs ≥ 10 and the player is on the losing-side underdog team, apply a mandatory -10% PTS confidence penalty regardless of Without-star elevation; if the resulting confidence drops below 75%, step down one tier.

RJ Barrett PTS MISS (13 vs threshold 15): The tier_walk correctly identified that VOLATILE + 7/10 at T15 fires a hard skip, and the reasoning explicitly noted 'hard skip fires — volatile_weak_combo skip.' This pick was selected despite the hard skip rule being triggered, which is a selection_error. The human_verdict of 'keep' means the Analyst or human override retained it, but the model's own logic flagged it as a skip. With only 23 minutes played (compressed in a PHX blowout win of 22 points — TOR was the losing side of a BLOWOUT), Randle's 13 PTS fell 2 below threshold. Even absent the blowout context, the VOLATILE + 7/10 combination at T15 was a structural disqualifier. Lesson: VOLATILE + sub-8/10 hit rate at T15+ is a hard skip with no override pathway unless iron_floor is present; human 'keep' overrides on hard-skip picks should be flagged as audit violations. The blowout-loss context (TOR lost by 22) further confirms that retaining this pick was doubly wrong.

Brandon Ingram AST MISS (1 vs threshold 2): Ingram recorded only 1 assist in 27 minutes in a competitive PHX game (PHX won by 22 — actually a BLOWOUT, TOR lost by 22). With trend=down on AST and 27 minutes played in a blowout loss, Ingram's assist opportunities collapsed as TOR's offensive system broke down in the second half. The T2 AST=90% overall baseline is sound, but the blowout-loss game script for TOR — which was losing by a large margin — structurally suppressed half-court creation sequences, reducing assist volume below T2. This is a model_gap_rule miss: the TOR blowout-loss context (spread_abs=2.5 pre-game, but the actual margin was 22) was not predictable from pre-game data alone, but the trend=down signal on AST combined with a road game against a tough defense should have triggered a tier step-down to T1 or a confidence reduction below selection threshold. The pick also included a -5% tough-defense adjustment but did not apply a sufficient conservative discount for a road AST pick with downward trend. Lesson: for road AST picks with trend=down against tough defenses, apply an additional -5% conservative penalty (total -10% for trend+road+tough) before selecting; if the resulting confidence drops below 72%, skip the AST pick entirely.

Five picks carried human verdict trim or manual_skip flags — all five hit — but this is a one-session sample and does not invalidate the trim logic. The structural risks flagged (VOLATILE 3PM with blowout spread, B2B second-night PTS suppression at T10, blowout cap on secondary scorer PTS) remain valid bases for downward confidence adjustment. The system should not relax VOLATILE or blowout-cap penalties based on a single clean session; these flags exist to protect against sessions where all five marginal picks miss simultaneously.

The Jalen Johnson DNP (confirmed OUT pre-game) was handled correctly by the voiding workflow for the individual pick, but his inclusion as a parlay leg in 'Soft Slate Scorers' created a structural parlay exposure that was not surfaced in the parlay rationale. The parlay hit because two legs covered and the parlay result was marked HIT despite a null leg — but the Parlay Agent should explicitly flag any leg where the player has a recent OUT history or a same-day injury designation, and require confirmation of availability before finalizing the parlay.

The session was concentrated in the 70–75% confidence band (8 of 11 picks), with zero picks in the 81%+ bands. While 100% hit rate at 70–75% is a strong session result, the calibration data now shows a structural gap: the system is not generating high-confidence picks (81%+) on most slates, likely because blowout caps and VOLATILE penalties are systematically pulling qualifying picks down into the 70–75% band. The Analyst should review whether the blowout spread cap thresholds (≥12 → 74% ceiling) are set too aggressively for primary scorers in matchups where the spread reflects opponent depletion rather than genuine garbage-time risk.
recent_reinforcements: Scottie Barnes T10 PTS (10/10 consistent tag, 96% vs tough defenses) delivered 17 vs PHX — the consistent tag plus historically confirmed tough-defense performance is a reliable combination even when trend=down; the 10/10 hit rate made this the cleanest pick on the slate.

Low-threshold AST and 3PM picks on TOR players (Barrett T2 AST, Barrett T1 3PM) both hit in a competitive PHX game — the T2/T1 floor strategy for volatile players with directional risk correctly limits exposure while maintaining a qualifying tier, and the 75-80% confidence band went 2/2.

Jokic's iron-floor T20 PTS (22 actual vs 20 threshold) validated the elite-scorer blowout exemption logic in a DEN win by 16: despite a competitive-to-blowout margin, his 35 minutes of play confirmed that elite scorers are not rested mid-game at spreads in the 8–14 range unless the game is fully decided in the fourth quarter, and his volume held.

Randle REB T4 (9 actual vs 4 threshold) hit emphatically even in a 10-point road loss — the ultra-low REB floor strategy correctly isolated the most game-script-resilient prop type for a player on the losing side of a competitive game, confirming that rebounding volume is far less sensitive to blowout-loss game scripts than scoring or assists.

The 81-85% confidence band went 2/2 (Scottie Barnes PTS at 82%, Barnes REB at 83%), continuing the pattern that higher-confidence picks — when properly gated through the high-confidence gate conditions — outperform the 70-75% band. This reinforces the value of demanding all three gate conditions before assigning 81%+.

Depleted-opponent matchup stacking continued to deliver: Luka Doncic PTS 25 OVER vs. a DET squad missing Cunningham/Stewart/Sasser produced 32 PTS (39 min), and Banchero PTS 15 OVER vs. IND — the league's softest defense — exploded for 39 PTS in 37 min. When a primary scorer faces a confirmed-depleted opponent in a close game, the conservative low-threshold tier (T25 for Luka, T15 for Banchero) provided enormous cushion while still qualifying at the system's required hit rate.

Ultra-low AST floors (T4 at 4+ for both Luka and SGA) proved resilient across game scripts: Luka hit 6 AST in a close DET game and SGA hit 5 AST despite a confirmed blowout (OKC +20) where minute compression was a documented risk. The 85–90% hit rate at T4 AST for elite playmakers is validated as a durable signal even under blowout conditions, though the blowout cap correctly constrained confidence and kept these picks out of headline roles.

Iron-floor REB anchors at ultra-low thresholds (Barnes T4 REB, Bane T2 AST) provided clean hits on a B2B second night and a blowout spread respectively — both outcomes exceeded threshold by significant margins (Barnes 7 REB vs. 4 floor; Bane 7 AST vs. 2 floor). When iron-floor protection is confirmed at a low absolute threshold against a depleted frontcourt (UTAH missing Kessler/Nurkic), these function as near-locks and are ideal parlay anchors.

The system's human verdict trim/manual-skip decisions correctly identified the structurally marginal picks (SGA 3PM, SGA AST, Holmgren PTS, Banchero AST, Barnes PTS) — all five hit on the day, but the trim flags were process-correct given VOLATILE tags, blowout spreads, and B2B penalties. Do not update selection logic to include these types more aggressively; the hits on a single session do not override the structural reasons for the flags.

Luka's 3PM iron-floor pick (T3, actual = 3 — exact threshold hit) validated the HIT-on-exact-threshold grading rule and confirmed that iron-floor 3PM picks at low tiers can function as parlay anchors when the VOLATILE penalty is the only disqualifying factor. The system correctly classified this as a trim on process grounds (VOLATILE drops confidence below 75% floor), but the result reinforces that iron-floor protection at T3 for elite shooters with trend=up is statistically sound.
recent_recommendations: Implement a mandatory losing-side blowout PTS penalty of -10% when spread_abs ≥ 10 and the player is on the underdog team, applied regardless of Without-star elevation. Randle's PTS miss is the direct evidence: a 10/10 Without-Edwards baseline did not protect against a 10.5-point road underdog game script that compressed scoring to exactly one unit below the T10 threshold. If post-penalty confidence falls below 75%, step down one tier rather than proceeding at reduced confidence.

Enforce the VOLATILE + sub-8/10 hard skip at T15+ with zero human override pathway unless iron_floor is explicitly present on the picked stat. Barrett's PTS pick should never have appeared on the final card — the tier_walk correctly identified the hard skip, but it was retained anyway. Add an audit flag: any pick that fires a hard skip rule but appears in the final output without iron_floor override must be voided pre-game.

For road AST picks with trend=down against tough defenses, apply a stacked penalty of -10% total (trend=down: -5%, road+tough: -5%) before finalizing confidence. If the resulting confidence is below 72%, do not select the AST pick. Ingram's T2 AST miss in a TOR blowout loss demonstrates that downward trending assist volume on the road compounds with game-script compression — the pre-game confidence of 75% was already borderline and should have been reduced below selection threshold.

When the actual game margin significantly exceeds the pre-game spread (e.g., pre-game spread of 2.5 but actual margin of 22), this represents an unpredictable game-script outcome. However, for future sessions: when TOR (or any team) is playing a road game against a team with top-10 defensive rating AND the player has trend=down on the prop type, treat the combination as equivalent to a known BLOWOUT_RISK scenario and apply the -10% blowout penalty preemptively. The PHX game (TOR lost by 22) reinforces that tough-defense road games carry structural blowout risk even at small pre-game spreads.

The 70-75% confidence band went 5/8 (62.5%) against an expected 72.5%, underperforming by 10 percentage points. All three misses occurred in this band. Review whether picks in this band with two or more compounding risk factors (VOLATILE tag, trend=down, road underdog, tough defense) should be elevated to 76%+ requirements or skipped entirely. Consider a rule: picks in the 70-75% band require at least one positive signal (iron_floor, consistent tag, or 10/10 hit rate) to qualify; picks with only baseline qualifying hit rates (7/10 at T15, 9/10 at T10) plus two risk factors should be stepped down or skipped.

For Luka Doncic multi-prop stacking (PTS + AST), continue to use the T25 PTS / T4 AST combination when DET, WAS, or similarly depleted opponents appear — the 87% vs-tough PTS rate and 94% vs-tough AST rate provide structural justification. Do not upgrade to T30 PTS without confirming the T30 tier hit rate independently; the T25 tier produced 32 actual PTS with 7 units of cushion, validating tier selection.

For Scottie Barnes on B2B second nights: the T4 REB iron-floor pick is the correct primary selection (83% B2B hit rate, confirmed vs. depleted frontcourts), but the T10 PTS pick should be systematically deprioritized when the B2B rate falls to 75% and a blowout spread applies simultaneously — the double penalty (B2B suppression + blowout game-script) is not fully offset by the consistent tag alone. If both conditions are present, require B2B rate ≥80% at the selected PTS tier before including.

For SGA and Holmgren in games where OKC is favored by 15+: the blowout cap correctly constrained confidence to 74%, but the trim flags on SGA AST and Holmgren PTS were process-justified despite the hits. Maintain the current blowout cap rules for these player types. Do not add PHI as a 'soft' opponent bonus that overrides the blowout structure — the OKC blowout (won by 20) confirmed the structural risk was real even though stats cleared the thresholds.

For Paolo Banchero AST picks: the VOLATILE tag combined with the T4 floor and a blowout spread (−13.5 as home favorite) created a correctly identified manual-skip. Banchero's 6 actual AST was a hit on the day, but the skip was process-correct given 53% post-miss hit rate on VOLATILE AST. Re-evaluate Banchero AST only when VOLATILE tag is absent, trend is up, and the spread is ≤8 points — do not chase a single-session result here.

For the Parlay Agent: before finalizing any parlay leg, require a confirmed 'NOT_LISTED' injury status check for all players — not just at the time of pick generation, but as a pre-lock verification step. Jalen Johnson's OUT designation was known pre-game and correctly voided the individual pick, but the parlay leg was not removed. Implement a rule: any leg where injury_status_at_check is anything other than NOT_LISTED at lock time must be automatically removed from the parlay and replaced or the parlay must be reduced to a shorter card.
